## 2. The Hard Questions (tenure, title, company health)

cards.front: What does it mean to be successful in sales?
cards.back:
"Most reps would say quota attainment. I'd push back on that — quota is tactical. Hitting it in one quarter doesn't tell you if you built a repeatable engine or got lucky on a deal.
The way I'd define it: three layers. Tactical — hitting your number with high forecast accuracy. Strategic — building a book that compounds through renewals, expansion, and referrals. Personal — closing deals you're proud of, for buyers you respect. Hit all three for years and you're the rep everyone studies."
cards.backShort: Consistency, not heroics. A rep who hits 110% four quarters running with a clean, accurate forecast is worth far more than someone who sandbags three quarters and then lands one whale. Success to me is a book that's predictable — I hit my number, I call my forecast within a few points, and my customers renew and expand. That's why I track things like forecast accuracy, not just bookings. Anyone can get lucky once. Being reliable quarter after quarter is the actual job.

cards.front: What's your biggest achievement?
cards.back: Taking Hypertide from $30K MRR to $340K in 12 months as the only seller — but the achievement inside that story is the repositioning call. Killed the self-serve PLG motion in the first 60 days and replaced it with sales-led. I could have failed quietly — kept it alive, closed a few deals on the side, hit some intermediate number and nobody would have called it a failure. Instead I argued for tearing it down. That was a real risk on my part. The courage to make the call mattered more than the outcome.
cards.backShort: Taking Hypertide from $30K to $340K MRR in a year as the only seller — but the achievement I'm actually proud of is the repositioning underneath it. Anyone can grow a number in a good market. I had to tear out the existing self-serve motion, put a paid setup fee in front as a qualification gate when everyone thought adding friction was crazy, and prove that fewer, better-fit customers beat more signups. The 10x was the outcome. Having the conviction to break something that looked like it was working — that's the part.

cards.front: What's your biggest failure?
cards.back:
"Lost a six-figure deal at Hypertide because I'd never met the CFO. Had a strong champion who kept assuring me she'd brought the CFO along internally. When procurement showed up in the last week, the CFO came with three objections I'd never heard before — and the champion couldn't translate the value prop into her language. Deal died, closed three weeks later with a competitor.
What I changed — I don't leave a cycle now without meeting the economic buyer directly by the second discovery call. If the champion resists, I push harder, because that resistance is the deal-killer signal. The real failure wasn't the deal though — it was that I'd had a similar miss at RemoteLock a year earlier. I learned the lesson, but not fully. It took losing the second deal to actually change the behavior."
cards.backShort: The lost deal I mentioned — running a six-figure cycle through one champion with no economic buyer engaged, and watching it die when that champion moved on. It stung because I saw the risk and talked myself out of acting on it. But it's the most useful failure I've had, because it permanently rewired how I sell — multi-threading stopped being a best practice and became a rule. The four stakeholders on Willow Bridge exist because of that loss.

cards.front: You've never been an enterprise AE before. What makes you think you can do that?
cards.back:
Response 1:

"That question assumes the title 'Enterprise AE' is what makes someone capable of enterprise selling. It's not.

Most enterprise AEs with the title are order takers. They inherited a named account list, had SDRs feeding them qualified meetings, had SEs running the technical demo, had CSMs running the relationship post-close. The AE was the guy in the middle following up on emails and updating Salesforce. That's not a shot — that's just how most enterprise orgs are structured. The title doesn't tell you if someone can sell.

Here's what I've actually done. $300K ARR deal, seven-month cycle, five stakeholders including a CTO running a build-vs-buy objection I had to dismantle with a TCO analysis I built myself. Procurement. Legal. Held price. Multi-threaded the whole thing with no SE, no SDR, no support structure. At RemoteLock — 9 to 12 month cycles into VP of Ops and IT at large multifamily operators. Full committee, full legal review. 111% of an $800K quota.

The motion is enterprise. The title wasn't. That's the only gap.

And honestly — the real risk in this hire isn't me. It's the candidate who has 'Enterprise AE' on their resume because they worked at Salesforce and processed inbound leads into a mature territory for three years. That person has the credential and not the skill.

So — what specifically about enterprise AE experience are you worried I'm missing? Name it and I'll tell you whether it's a real gap or a proxy for something you can actually verify right now."

---

Response 2:

"Fair question. Let me push back on the framing a little.

I closed a $300K ARR deal at Hypertide. Seven-month cycle, five stakeholders, procurement, legal, a build-vs-buy objection from a CTO. That's an enterprise sale. The startup title doesn't change what the motion looked like. At RemoteLock I ran 9-12 month cycles into multifamily — VP of Ops, IT, procurement, regional management. Same thing. Enterprise motion, startup wrapper.

What I haven't done is carry a named enterprise territory at a company where that's the only thing I do. That's the gap. I own that.

But here's what I'd ask — what actually makes an enterprise AE? Is it the title, or is it the ability to navigate a complex buying committee, build a business case, hold price, and close? Because I've done all of that. The reps who struggle in enterprise are the ones who've never multi-threaded, never built a TCO, never lost a deal because they didn't find the CFO early enough. I've done all three.

The learning curve for me isn't 'how does enterprise work.' It's learning your specific buyers, your specific objection patterns, your specific competitive landscape. That's a 90-day problem, not a career problem."
cards.backShort:
Fair, and I'll meet it head on. No, I haven't had "Enterprise AE" on a business card. But enterprise selling is a set of muscles, not a title, and I've been using them — six-figure deals, multiple stakeholders, long cycles, and a real business case. Willow Bridge was a $96K deal across twelve regions and four decision-makers. ColdIQ was a $300K annual contract where I had to win a build-versus-buy argument against a technical CTO. That's enterprise motion; it just wasn't labeled that way. Enterprise is more of what I already do well — more stakeholders, bigger business cases, longer patience — not a different skill I've never touched. Give me the seat and I'll show you the muscle's already there.

## 9. "Why This Company" Variants

cards.front: How do you negotiate?
cards.back:
The honest answer is that I try to win the negotiation before it ever happens. By the time we're talking price, I've already done the work — I know the cost of their problem in dollars, I know who actually signs, and I know their timeline. If I've quantified the pain correctly in discovery, price stops being the conversation. So most of my "negotiation" is really disciplined qualification upfront.

When we do get to terms, my rule is simple: I never give a concession, I trade one. If a buyer asks for something, my reflex is "I can probably do that — what can you do for me?" Multi-year, prepayment, a faster signature, a case study, a reference call. Every give has a get attached. That keeps the deal balanced and, more importantly, it protects the relationship after the ink dries, because nobody respects a vendor who folds the second they're pushed.
cards.backShort: Honestly, I try to win the negotiation before it starts — if I've quantified the pain in discovery and I know who signs and what the deadline is, price stops being the fight. When we do get to terms, my rule is I never give a concession, I trade one. If they ask for something, my reflex is "I can probably do that — what can you do for me?" Multi-year, prepay, a faster signature, a reference. Every give has a get. That keeps the deal balanced and it keeps their respect after we sign — nobody trusts a vendor who folds the second they're pushed.

cards.front: Do you ever give discounts?
cards.back:
Yes — but never to win a deal, and never on the first ask. If I discount just to close, I've told the buyer two things: my original price was fake, and pressure works on me. Both of those cost me on the renewal and the expansion.

So when I discount, it's a trade and it's tied to something that's good for the business — a longer commitment, payment upfront, scope I'm comfortable removing, or timing that helps our quarter. The discount has a reason the buyer can see, which is what keeps the price credible. "I'll give you 10% because you asked" erodes trust. "I can get to that number if we go two years and you sign by Friday" builds it.
cards.backShort: Yes — but never just to win a deal, and never on the first ask. If I discount to close, I've told the buyer two things: my price was fake, and pressure works on me. Both of those cost me at renewal and expansion. So when I discount, it's a trade tied to a reason they can see — longer term, cash upfront, tighter scope. "Ten percent because you asked" kills trust. "I can hit that number if we go two years and you sign Friday" builds it.

cards.front: Walk me through an example where you gave a discount and why.
cards.back:
I had a mid-market deal where the buyer loved the product but came back saying the annual number was above what they'd budgeted for the year. The lazy move is to just knock the price down. Instead I isolated it: was it the total cost, or the cash hit this fiscal year? It was the cash timing.

So I traded. I offered roughly a 12% reduction in exchange for a two-year commitment with year one paid upfront. That solved their actual problem — predictable spend they'd locked budget around — and it solved mine: I doubled the contract length, de-risked the renewal, and pulled cash forward. The discount wasn't a giveaway, it was the price of a better deal for both sides. The "why" is the whole point — I never want a discount floating around with no logic attached to it, because that's the number they'll expect again next year.
cards.backShort: Mid-market deal, buyer loved the product but said the annual number was over budget. Lazy move is to just knock the price down. Instead I isolated it — was it total cost, or cash this fiscal year? It was cash timing. So I traded: about 12% off in exchange for a two-year commitment with year one paid upfront. That solved their real problem — predictable spend they'd already budgeted for — and it solved mine, because I doubled the term and pulled cash forward. The discount bought me a better deal. It wasn't a giveaway.

cards.front: How do you handle objections?
cards.back:
My starting assumption is that an objection is usually a signal I missed something in discovery, not a wall. So step one is I don't get defensive and I don't start pitching — I get curious and isolate it. "Totally fair — when you say it's too expensive, is it the price relative to budget, relative to a competitor, or relative to the value you're seeing?" Those are three completely different problems with three different answers, and most reps lose deals answering the wrong one.

Once I've isolated the real objection, I reframe it against the cost of doing nothing. If their problem is costing them  a month, sitting still isn't free — staying put is the most expensive option on the table. And then I'll often test whether it's the actual blocker: "If we solved this, is there anything else standing between us and moving forward?" That surfaces the hidden objection fast, so I'm not whacking moles one at a time. The goal isn't to "overcome" them like it's combat — it's to get to the truth quickly so we both know whether this is real.
cards.backShort: My starting assumption is that an objection means I missed something in discovery, not that it's a wall — so I get curious, not defensive. And I isolate before I answer. "Too expensive" is three different problems: too expensive versus budget, versus a competitor, or versus the value they're seeing. Each one has a different answer, and if I respond before I know which it is, I'm guessing. Once I've isolated it, I reframe against the cost of doing nothing — because if their problem's bleeding money, sitting still isn't free. The goal isn't to win the objection, it's to get to the truth fast.

cards.front: What's your first 30/60/90 (short version)?
cards.back: 30 — learn the product, the buyer, and the top reps cold. 60 — running my own discovery and territory plan. 90 — self-sourced pipeline moving and first deals in flight. I ramp on process, not luck.
cards.backShort: 30 — learn the product, the buyer, and the top reps cold. 60 — running my own discovery and territory plan. 90 — self-sourced pipeline moving and first deals in flight. I ramp on process, not luck.
